questions.question: Define an array in C programming and explain why arrays are called homogeneous data structures.
keyPoints: Array is a collection of elements of the same data type stored in contiguous memory locations
Homogeneous means all elements must be of the same type (e.g., all int, all char)
Elements are accessed using index numbers
Single variable name represents multiple values

questions.question: Explain the difference between the following array declarations: int arr1[5]; int arr2[5] = {1,2,3}; int arr3[] = {1,2,3,4,5};
keyPoints: arr1[5] declares array of 5 integers with uninitialized values (garbage for local, zero for global/static)
arr2[5] = {1,2,3} partially initializes—first 3 get 1,2,3 and remaining get 0
arr3[] without size gets automatic size deduced from initializer list (5 elements)
All three create arrays of 5 integers

questions.question: What is the output of the following code? Explain why. int arr[5] = {10, 20, 30}; printf("%d %d %d", arr[0], arr[3], sizeof(arr)/sizeof(arr[0]));
keyPoints: arr[0] = 10 (first element)
arr[3] = 0 (partial initialization fills rest with 0)
sizeof(arr)/sizeof(arr[0]) = 20/4 = 5 (number of elements)
Output: 10 0 5

questions.question: Why is random access in arrays O(1)? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Elements stored in contiguous memory locations
Formula: Address = Base Address + (Index × Element Size)
No need to search—direct calculation gives element address
Example: For int arr[10] at address 1000, arr[5] is at 1000 + (5×4) = 1020

questions.question: What are the limitations of arrays in C programming?
keyPoints: Fixed size: size must be known at compile time (except VLA)
No bounds checking: accessing invalid index causes undefined behavior
Cannot store mixed data types: all elements must be same type
Insertion/deletion expensive: requires shifting elements

questions.question: Write a C program to initialize an integer array with 5 elements and display all elements using a for loop.
keyPoints: Include stdio.h
Declare and initialize array: int arr[5] = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50};
Use for loop with index from 0 to 4
Print each element using printf with arr[i]

questions.question: Explain the concept of row-major order in two-dimensional arrays with an example.
keyPoints: Row-major order stores elements row by row sequentially
For int arr[2][3]: elements stored as arr[0][0], arr[0][1], arr[0][2], arr[1][0], arr[1][1], arr[1][2]
All 6 elements occupy contiguous memory locations
Memory address calculation: Base + (row × columns + col) × elementSize

questions.question: Write a C program to find the largest and smallest element in a one-dimensional array of 10 integers. Explain your logic step by step.
keyPoints: Initialize array with 10 elements
Assume first element is both largest and smallest (max = arr[0], min = arr[0])
Traverse array from index 1 to 9
Compare each element with max—if greater, update max
Compare each element with min—if smaller, update min
After loop, print max and min values
Time complexity: O(n) - single pass through array

questions.question: Explain the relationship between array name and pointer in C. Write a program demonstrating pointer arithmetic with arrays.
keyPoints: Array name without brackets acts as pointer to first element
arr == &arr[0] (both give address of first element)
Pointer arithmetic: adding 1 to pointer moves to next element
*(arr+i) is equivalent to arr[i]
Address increments by sizeof(element_type)
Program should show: printing arr, &arr[0], using pointer to traverse

questions.question: What is array bounds checking? Why doesn't C perform it? Write about the consequences of accessing out-of-bounds elements.
keyPoints: Bounds checking verifies index is within valid range (0 to size-1)
C does NOT perform automatic bounds checking for performance reasons
Programmer has full responsibility to ensure valid indices
Out-of-bounds access causes: undefined behavior, segmentation faults, garbage values, security vulnerabilities
Example: int arr[5]; arr[5] = 10; is invalid but C may not warn

questions.question: Write a C program to calculate the sum and average of N elements entered by the user. Explain the steps involved.
keyPoints: Ask user to input value of N
Declare array of size N using Variable Length Array (VLA) or dynamic allocation
Use loop to input N elements using scanf
Initialize sum = 0, then add each element in loop
Calculate average = (float)sum / N
Print sum and average with appropriate formatting
Handle edge case: N should be greater than 0
